title: Beni Hasan

infobox.value: (also written as **Bani Hasan**, or also **Beni-Hassan**) ([Arabic](/wiki/Arabic_language "Arabic language"): بني حسن) is an [ancient Egyptian](/wiki/Ancient_Egypt "Ancient Egypt") cemetery. It is located approximately 20 kilometers (12 mi) to the south of modern-day [Minya](/wiki/Minya,_Egypt "Minya, Egypt") in the region known as [Middle Egypt](/wiki/Middle_Egypt "Middle Egypt"), the area between [Asyut](/wiki/Asyut "Asyut") and [Memphis](/wiki/Memphis,_Egypt "Memphis, Egypt").[[1]](#cite_note-bm-1): 120
Richards, Janet. *Society And Death In Ancient Egypt.* Cambridge, United Kingdom: Cambridge UP, 2005
Garstang, John. *The Burial Customs of Ancient Egypt*. London, England: Archibald Constable & Co Ltd, 1907
Kamrin, Janice. *The Cosmos of Khnumhotep II at Beni Hasan.* London, England: Kegan Paul International, 1999.
[Bard, Kathryn A.](/wiki/Kathryn_A._Bard "Kathryn A. Bard") *An Introduction To The Archaeology Of Ancient Egypt.* Oxford, United Kingdom: Blackwell Ltd, 2008
Betany Hughes *Nile : Egypt's Great River.* BBC TV 2019

Mieroop, Marc Van De (2010). [*A History of Ancient Egypt*](https://books.google.com/books?id=JADDYAZ9GIIC&pg=PA131). John Wiley & Sons. p. 131. [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [978-1-118-89611-2](/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-1-118-89611-2 "Special:BookSources/978-1-118-89611-2").

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Village and archaeological site in Middle Egypt

For other uses, see [Bani Hasan (disambiguation)](/wiki/Bani_Hasan_(disambiguation) "Bani Hasan (disambiguation)").

Beni Hasan

**Beni Hasan** (also written as **Bani Hasan**, or also **Beni-Hassan**) ([Arabic](/wiki/Arabic_language "Arabic language"): بني حسن) is an [ancient Egyptian](/wiki/Ancient_Egypt "Ancient Egypt") cemetery. It is located approximately 20 kilometers (12 mi) to the south of modern-day [Minya](/wiki/Minya,_Egypt "Minya, Egypt") in the region known as [Middle Egypt](/wiki/Middle_Egypt "Middle Egypt"), the area between [Asyut](/wiki/Asyut "Asyut") and [Memphis](/wiki/Memphis,_Egypt "Memphis, Egypt").[[1]](#cite_note-bm-1): 120

While there are some [Old Kingdom](/wiki/Old_Kingdom "Old Kingdom") burials at the site, it was primarily used during the [Middle Kingdom](/wiki/Middle_Kingdom_of_Egypt "Middle Kingdom of Egypt"), spanning the 21st to 17th centuries [BCE](/wiki/BCE "BCE") ([Middle Bronze Age](/wiki/Middle_Bronze_Age "Middle Bronze Age")).[[2]](#cite_note-Robins-2): 8

To the south of the cemetery is a temple constructed by [Hatshepsut](/wiki/Hatshepsut "Hatshepsut") and [Thutmose III](/wiki/Thutmose_III "Thutmose III"), dedicated to the local goddess [Pakhet](/wiki/Pakhet "Pakhet").[[1]](#cite_note-bm-1): 128 It is known as the [Cave of Artemis](/wiki/Speos_Artemidos "Speos Artemidos"), because the [Greeks](/wiki/Ancient_Greece "Ancient Greece") identified Pakhet with [Artemis](/wiki/Artemis "Artemis"), and the temple is subterranean.

## Cemetery

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Beni_Hasan&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Cemetery")]

Provincial governors in the Middle Kingdom continued to be buried in decorated [rock-cut tombs](/wiki/Rock-cut_tombs "Rock-cut tombs") in their local cemeteries, carried over from the [First Intermediate Period](/wiki/First_Intermediate_Period "First Intermediate Period"), at sites such as Beni Hasan.[[2]](#cite_note-Robins-2): 101 There is evidence of a reorganization of the system of government during the [12th Dynasty](/wiki/12th_Dynasty "12th Dynasty"). During the First Intermediate Period and for some of the Middle Kingdom period it was common for [nomarchs](/wiki/Nomarch "Nomarch") (someone who oversees/controls a government specified area) to be hereditary positions; the elite did not depend on the king to legitimize their power as much as they had in the Old Kingdom. In the 12th Dynasty the power of the nomarchs began to be curtailed, and provincial governors were appointed or at least confirmed by the king.

There are 39 ancient tombs here of [Middle Kingdom](/wiki/Middle_Kingdom_of_Egypt "Middle Kingdom of Egypt") (c. 21st to 19th centuries BCE) nomarchs of the [Oryx nome](/wiki/Oryx_nome "Oryx nome"), who governed from [Hebenu](/wiki/Hebenu "Hebenu"). Due to the quality of, and distance to the cliffs in the west, these tombs were constructed on the east bank. There is a spatial distribution in this cemetery (there are two cemeteries here: the upper range and the lower necropolis) associated with the different levels of resources available to the deceased; the most important people were buried near the top of the cliff.[[3]](#cite_note-3): 80 In the lower cemetery there are 888 shaft tombs, dating to the Middle Kingdom, that were excavated by [John Garstang](/wiki/John_Garstang "John Garstang"); for the most part these tombs shared a similar general design which included a small chamber or recess at the foot of the shaft (facing south) to receive the coffin and the funeral deposits.[[4]](#cite_note-4): 45

In the upper cemetery members of the elite class built striking tombs to represent their social and political positions as the rulers and officials of the Oryx Nome, which is the 16th Nome of Upper Egypt. At this site, the provincial high elite were buried in large and elaborately decorated tombs carved into the limestone cliffs near the provincial capital, located in the upper cemetery area. These tombs lie in a row on a north–south axis. There is a slight break in the natural rock terrace, on to which they open, that divides the thirty-nine high status tombs into two groups.[[5]](#cite_note-5): 25 The basic design of these elite tombs was an outer court and a rock-cut pillared room (sometimes referred to as the chapel) in which there was a shaft that led to the burial chamber.

Some of the larger tombs have biographical inscriptions and were painted with scenes of daily life and warfare. They are famous for the quality of their paintings. Many of these scenes are in poor condition, though in the 19th century copies were made of several of them.[[6]](#cite_note-6): 189 Howard Carter as a teenager 1891 spent a season painting watercolours of some of them.[[7]](#cite_note-7) In 2020, [Martin Bommas](/wiki/Martin_Bommas "Martin Bommas") identified all four walls of the [First Intermediate Period](/wiki/First_Intermediate_Period "First Intermediate Period") burial chamber of the governor Baqet II in tomb BH33 as being inscribed with [Pyramid Texts](/wiki/Pyramid_Texts "Pyramid Texts") and [Coffin Texts](/wiki/Coffin_Texts "Coffin Texts") in the [Hieratic](/wiki/Hieratic "Hieratic") script which served the tomb owner as a ritual handbook,[[8]](#cite_note-8) published in 2022. This discovery marked a breakthrough in the understanding of the transmission of [Pyramid Texts](/wiki/Pyramid_Texts "Pyramid Texts") after the downfall of the [Old Kingdom](/wiki/Old_Kingdom "Old Kingdom").

## Notable tombs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Beni_Hasan&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Notable tombs")]

West Asiatic visitors to Egypt (c.1900 BCE)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f4/Procession_of_the_Aamu%2C_Tomb_of_Khnumhotep_II_%28composite%29.jpg/500px-Procession_of_the_Aamu%2C_Tomb_of_Khnumhotep_II_%28composite%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Procession_of_the_Aamu,_Tomb_of_Khnumhotep_II_(composite).jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/29/Drawing_of_the_procession_of_the_Aamu_group_tomb_of_Khnumhotep_II_at_Beni_Hassan.jpg/500px-Drawing_of_the_procession_of_the_Aamu_group_tomb_of_Khnumhotep_II_at_Beni_Hassan.jpg)](/wiki/File:Drawing_of_the_procession_of_the_Aamu_group_tomb_of_Khnumhotep_II_at_Beni_Hassan.jpg)

A group of West Asiatic foreigners visiting the Egyptian official [Khnumhotep II](/wiki/Khnumhotep_II "Khnumhotep II") circa 1900 BC. The leader is labelled a "[Hyksos](/wiki/Hyksos "Hyksos")". Tomb of [12th-dynasty](/wiki/12th_dynasty "12th dynasty") official [Khnumhotep II](/wiki/Khnumhotep_II "Khnumhotep II"), at Beni Hasan.[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/09/Beni_Hassan_tomb_15_wrestling_detail.jpg/330px-Beni_Hassan_tomb_15_wrestling_detail.jpg)](/wiki/File:Beni_Hassan_tomb_15_wrestling_detail.jpg)

Detail of the wrestling scenes in tomb 15

Four out of the 39 tombs are accessible to the public. Notable tombs are:

* Tomb 2 – [Amenemhat, known as Ameny](/wiki/Amenemhat_(BH2) "Amenemhat (BH2)"), nomarch under [Senusret I](/wiki/Senusret_I "Senusret I") (accessible).
* Tomb 3 – [Khnumhotep II](/wiki/Khnumhotep_II "Khnumhotep II"), notable for the depiction of caravans of [Semitic](/wiki/Semitic_people "Semitic people") traders (accessible).
* Tomb 4 – [Khnumhotep IV](/wiki/Khnumhotep_IV "Khnumhotep IV"), nomarch during the late [12th Dynasty](/wiki/12th_Dynasty "12th Dynasty") (closed).
* Tomb 13 – Khnumhotep, royal scribe during the 12th Dynasty (closed).
* Tomb 14 – [Khnumhotep I](/wiki/Khnumhotep_I "Khnumhotep I"), nomarch under [Amenemhat I](/wiki/Amenemhat_I "Amenemhat I") (closed).
* Tomb 15 – [Baqet III](/wiki/Baqet_III "Baqet III"), notable for the depiction of [wrestling](/wiki/Wrestling "Wrestling") techniques and is among the [earliest known portrayals from Antiquity](/wiki/History_of_wrestling#Antiquity "History of wrestling").
* Tomb 17 – [Khety](/wiki/Khety_(BH17) "Khety (BH17)"), nomarch during the [11th Dynasty](/wiki/11th_Dynasty "11th Dynasty"), son of Baqet; notable for depiction of what may be ball games (accessible).
* Tomb 21 – [Nakht](/wiki/Nakht_(BH21) "Nakht (BH21)"), nomarch during the 12th Dynasty (closed).
* Tomb 23 – [Netjernakht](/wiki/Netjernakht "Netjernakht"), *overseer of the [Eastern Desert](/wiki/Eastern_Desert "Eastern Desert")* during the 12th dynasty (closed).
* Tomb 27 – [Ramushenti](/wiki/Ramushenti "Ramushenti"), nomarch during the 11th Dynasty (closed).
* Tomb 29 – [Baqet I](/wiki/Baqet_I "Baqet I"), nomarch during the 11th Dynasty (closed).
* Tomb 33 – [Baqet II](/wiki/Baqet_II "Baqet II"), nomarch during the 11th Dynasty (closed).
